summary: Jeff Atwood celebrates Visual Studio .NET 2005's decision to enable Option Explicit by default, which prevents the compiler from silently creating new variables when programmers make typos. He argues that Option Explicit Off is 'pure, unmitigated evil' that has caused real problems in production projects. The post also discusses Option Strict, which enforces strong type checking and prevents automatic type coercion. While Atwood agrees Option Strict should probably be on by default, he acknowledges it has legitimate uses for late binding code, unlike Option Explicit Off which has no valid use case. He notes that partial classes in VB.NET 2.0 allow developers to selectively disable Option Strict for specific code sections. Ultimately, he's grateful for the Explicit default change even if Microsoft didn't go far enough with Strict.
key_points: Option Explicit Off allows the compiler to silently create new variables on typos, leading to bugs that are extremely difficult to track down
Option Strict Off enables automatic type coercion which can cause obscure bugs and generates slower IL code (hence 'Option Slow')
Default settings are incredibly powerful - developers often don't realize when dangerous options are enabled months into a project
VB.NET 2.0's partial classes allow granular control, enabling Option Strict Off only for sections that need late binding
Option Explicit Off has no legitimate use, while Option Strict Off has valid use cases for late binding code
money_quotes: There's nothing more vicious than making an innocent typo when referencing a variable and not knowing about it because the compiler silently declares a new variable for you.
Just talking about it makes my eye twitch uncontrollably.
Option Explicit Off is pure, unmitigated evil.
Laugh all you want, but this is the power of default values.
What I really needed was an option not to work with knuckleheads who turn it off.
I consider it a minor miracle that we even got Explicit. I'll take it.
key_insight: Default settings in development tools are extraordinarily powerful because developers often don't examine them, making safe defaults a moral imperative for tool designers.
